coredata.dc:title: Dual-emitting film with cellulose nanocrystal-assisted carbon dots grafted SrAl2O4, Eu2+, Dy3+ phosphors for temperature sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Longfei
Lyu, Shaoyi
Zhang, Qijun
Wu, Yuntao
Melcher, Chuck
Chmely, Stephen C.
Chen, Zhilin
Wang, Siqun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we synthesized a novel dual-emitting fluorescent phosphor from cellulose nanocrystal (CNC)-assisted carbon dots (CDs)-grafted SrAl2O4, Eu2+, Dy3+ (SAO) through a facile core-shell process. The CNC-CDs-coated SAO presents excellent scattered dual-emission and improved water resistance without destruction of the SrAl2O4 crystals. The phosphors were then reacted with coupling amino-silane and assembled with nanofibrillated cellulose skeletons to create flexible isotropic films. The obtained phosphors and hybrid films were characterized via electron microscopy, photoluminescence analysis, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results demonstrate that the optical signals of phosphors can be controlled by CDs content. The assembled cellulose films exhibit strong temperature responses, high light-induced scattering, and good flexibility. The luminescent emission of films is highly sensitive to surrounding temperature variation (243–383 K) and good linearity behavior was obtained for such a sensitive sensor. Such flexible nanofibrillated cellulose films are excellent candidates for temperature sensor devices in industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Temperature
Film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of ONFC film with CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors
Fabrication of temperature-sensing films via solution dipping
Characterization and optical properties of fluorescent phosphors
Formation of fluorescent hybrid ONFC sensing films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds of the Chinese Academy of Forestry for Wood Composites and Chemical Utilization Program
USDA National Institute of Food and Agriculture
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Institute of Food and Agriculture

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds of the Chinese Academy of Forestry for Wood Composites and Chemical Utilization Program ( CAFYBB2017ZX003 ), as well as the USDA National Institute of Food and Agriculture (Hatch Project 1012359). The authors also acknowledge financial support from the China Scholarship Council .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CNC pre-dispersed CDs-grafted SAO dual-emitting phosphors and preparation of flexible ONFC fluorescent scattering films for temperature sensing.

Microscopic morphologies (SEM, TEM images) of the SAO phosphors with and without modification: (a) uncoated SAO particles; (b) silica sol with CNC pre-dispersed CDs-coated SAO (CNC-CDs@SAO); (c) APTMS-grafted CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors (NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO). Inset graphs show the TEM images in rainbow colors to visualize the coated surface.

FTIR spectra of (a) SAO, CNC pre-dispersed CDs-modified SAO (CNC-CDs@SAO), and APTMS-modified CNC-CDs@SAO particles (NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO), as well as (b) CNCs and CDs. (c) X-ray diffraction patterns for SAO, CNC-CDs@SAO, and NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO, as well as CNCs. (d) Zeta potentials of modified particles, ONFC, CNC dispersant, and CDs.

(a) XPS spectra of SAO phosphors before and after modification via CNC pre-dispersed CDs and APTMS. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectra and fitting results for the (b) CNC-CDs@SAO and (c) NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors are also shown. (d) High-resolution Si 2p XPS spectra of coated particles, and (e) TGA and (f) differential thermogravimetry (DTG) curves of SAO phosphors before and after coating modifications.

(a) Excitation spectrum of SAO phosphors (detected at 512-nm) and emission spectrum of CDs (irritated at 365-nm); (b) PL spectra of SAO particles, CDs, and CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors with a relative mass ratio of 2.0 (excited at 365-nm); (c) PL spectra of CNC-CDs@SAO particles with different CNC-CDs incorporations in increasing mass ratios (CNC-CDs/SAO = 0, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0); (d) fluorescent intensity of SAO phosphors before and after modifications.

(a) Optical signal transfer mechanism of the CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors and their possible transfer process through an energy band structure. (b) Decay curves of CNC-CDs@SAO samples (CNC-CDs/SAO mass ratio of 2.0) in comparison to CNC-coated SAO (CNC@SiO2@SAO) and SiO2-coated SAO phosphors (SiO2@SAO). (c) CIE chromaticity diagrams (Ex = 370-nm) for SAO phosphors (a#, b#, and c# refer to SAO, SiO2@SAO, and CNC@SiO2@SAO), CDs solutions, and CNC-CDs@SAO samples with different CDs ratios, where samples 1# through 5# correspond to mass ratios (CNC-CDs/SAO) of 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, and 3.0, respectively. Inset photos show the CNC-CDs@SAO, CDs, and SAO aqueous suspensions, excited under a 365-nm UV lamp.

Typical micromorphology of modified SAO particles in ONFC substrate film: SEM images of the in-plane and through-thickness of (a, d) ONFC film (inset: high-magnification micrographs of hybrid film), (b, e) fluorescent film with CNC-CDs@SAO (mass ratio = 5/5), and (c, f) sensing film with NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO (mass ratio = 5/5). Also shown are schematic models of the (g) pure ONFC film and (h, i) fluorescence films with distinct coated SAO particles.

(a) Digital photographs of phosphors blended with water and ONFC dispersion in daylight (left) and darkness (right), as well as the light translucence of the films with amino-coated particles in different mass ratios (ONFC/phosphors). (b) Optical transmittance of ONFC substrate film and ONFC sensing films with amino-coated particles (ONFC/phosphors). Comparative laser scattering behavior of (c) ONFC film and (d) ONFC sensing film with amino-coated particles (ONFC/phosphors = 5/5). (e) Schematic diagram of the flexible dual-emitting ONFC sensing film.

Fluorescent temperature-dependent spectra of (a) directly ONFC sensing films with NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors (ONFC/phosphor = 5/5) and (b) corresponding CDs-dipping ONFC sensing films in the range of 243–383 K (thermal gradient of 20 °C, Ex = 370-nm). Inset photos show the luminescent films at 243 and 383 K using 365-nm UV irritation. (c) PL intensity ratio of SAO (506-nm) signal to CDs (452-nm) under different temperature and fitted curve. (d) Temperature-dependent reversibility of ONFC sensing film in the range of 243–383 K.

head.ce:title: Dual-emitting film with cellulose nanocrystal-assisted carbon dots grafted SrAl2O4, Eu2+, Dy3+ phosphors for temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Wood Industry of Chinese Academy of Forestry, Hunan Collaborative Innovation Center for Effective Utilizing of Wood & Bamboo Resources, Beijing, 100091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Wood Industry of Chinese Academy of Forestry
Hunan Collaborative Innovation Center for Effective Utilizing of Wood & Bamboo Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Renewable Carbon, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, 37996, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Scintillation Materials Research Center, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN, 37996, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we synthesized a novel dual-emitting fluorescent phosphor from cellulose nanocrystal (CNC)-assisted carbon dots (CDs)-grafted SrAl2O4, Eu2+, Dy3+ (SAO) through a facile core-shell process. The CNC-CDs-coated SAO presents excellent scattered dual-emission and improved water resistance without destruction of the SrAl2O4 crystals. The phosphors were then reacted with coupling amino-silane and assembled with nanofibrillated cellulose skeletons to create flexible isotropic films. The obtained phosphors and hybrid films were characterized via electron microscopy, photoluminescence analysis, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results demonstrate that the optical signals of phosphors can be controlled by CDs content. The assembled cellulose films exhibit strong temperature responses, high light-induced scattering, and good flexibility. The luminescent emission of films is highly sensitive to surrounding temperature variation (243–383 K) and good linearity behavior was obtained for such a sensitive sensor. Such flexible nanofibrillated cellulose films are excellent candidates for temperature sensor devices in industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature detection is an important research area in industrial engineering, biomedical monitoring, and environmental regulation systems, where accurate temperature information is required to ensure that systems operate efficiently and reliably (Belkhir, Toncelli, Parchur, Alves, & Maalej, 2017; Dinh et al., 2015; Xie et al., 2018). Conventional thermocouple temperature sensors operate based on heat flow through invasive probes (Lei et al., 2015), which are provide relatively low accuracy and are susceptible to being influenced by interfering signals, resulting in major measurement errors, especially in applications with high voltages, strong electromagnetic fields, and microwave radiation (Saccomandi, Schena, & Silvestri, 2013). Recently, temperature fluorescence-based phosphors, such as rare-earth materials (Donmez, Oktem, & Yilmaz, 2018), semiconductors (Matsuda et al., 2013), and carbon dots (CDs), have been widely studied based on their temperature-induced dependencies (Liang et al., 2014). The fluorescence intensity of these materials can be utilized to reflect the temperature variations. Therefore, the development of temperature-sensitive luminescent materials has attracted significant interest in the fabrication of temperature sensors.

SrAl2O4, Eu2+, Dy3+ (SAO) phosphors in micro- and nano-particle sizes are widely used in displays, sensing, and radiation dosimetry (Khattab, Abou-Yousef, & Kamel, 2018). They were initially introduced into temperature-sensing areas for their high fluorescent intensity. However, SAO particles present relatively narrow wavelength emission under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, which is easily disturbed by external fluctuations. Therefore, it is necessary to introduce additional fluorescent signals to improve the adaptability and accuracy of such sensors (Li et al., 2017). However, SAO phosphors easily hydrolyze in water environments, resulting in the destruction of the crystalline structure of SrAl2O4 and a non-recoverable fluorescent quenching phenomenon. Therefore, to broaden the application of SAO phosphors, it is necessary to modify them for improved water resistance and compatibility with affiliated luminescent signals.

Fluorescent CDs as a new type of nanomaterial has been rapidly developed in recent years (Li et al., 2018; Feng et al., 2017). Compared to traditional semiconductor dots and organic pigments (Khattab, Rehan, & Hamouda, 2018), CDs provide numerous advantages, such as high fluorescent intensity, good optical stability, as well as easily functionalized surface (Sun & Wu, 2018). They are widely used in bio-imaging, photo catalysis, and sensing applications (Sarkar et al., 2018). Because of an overlap between CDs emission and the excitation spectrum of SAO phosphors, there is a possibility of utilizing SAO and CDs to construct dual-emitting particles. Researchers reported phosphors combining organic silane-CDs and inorganic particles and revealed their application to fluorescent oxygen (Wang, Zhang, Zhou, Liu, & Lei, 2016) and temperature sensing (Lei et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016). Our previous report (Zhang, Lyu, Chen, & Wang, 2018a) also confirmed that TEOS modification can effectively improve the hydrolytic resistance of SAO phosphors and maintain over 90% of their fluorescent intensity. However, the CDs in these studies tended to agglomerate because of rich surface of silicon hydroxyl groups, resulting in partial fluorescent extinction of the CDs. Therefore, in this study, we introduced cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs), which are rod-like nanofibers with excellent light transmission and scattering performance (Miyagi & Teramoto, 2018), to disperse CDs, which can effectively restrain the aggregation of CDs and enhance the light-capturing ability of SAO phosphors, leading to better dual-emitting effects.

Based on the fluorescent phosphors, there is an urgent demand for developing flexible film devices with stable luminescent signals and superior bonding interfaces. Conventional sensor films are constructed from spray-deposited (Mani & Rayappan, 2013) or coated phosphors on glass sheets (León-Luis, Rodríguez-Mendoza, Lalla, & Lavín, 2011). These sensors are relatively large in size, brittle, and less prone to bend under external forces. Therefore, some researchers have developed flexible sensors with polymer substrates, such as polyethylene terephthalate (Pang et al., 2018) and polymethylmethacrylate (Wang, Yang, Zhang, Song, & Wong, 2017). Compared to non-biodegradable plastics, nanofibrillated cellulose is a green biomass substrate with a high aspect ratio, excellent mechanical strength, and biodegradability (Wu et al., 2018; Zhang, Li, Shi, Chen, & Fan, 2018). In general, it is a promising candidate to replace petroleum-based polymers (Wang et al., 2018). Additionally, nanocellulose has attracted significant interest in the development of flexible composites in the fields of functional coatings (Liu, Chen, Huang, & Lai, 2016), transparent (or semitransparent) films (Zhang et al., 2018a), and flexible optoelectronic devices (Zhao et al., 2014).

The goal of this study was to fabricate flexible dual-emitting films with CNC-assisted CDs-grafted SAO phosphors as functional fillers and nanofibrillated cellulose as a reinforcing substrate. To effectively disperse the CDs and enhance the light-capturing ability as well as hydrolytic resistance of SAO phosphors, the strategy for CNC pre-dispersed CDs was applied and incorporated with SAO particles utilizing a sol-gel method. Furthermore, (3-aminopropyl) trimethoxy-silane (APTMS) amino-grafted modification was utilized to modify the surface characteristics of the CDs-coated SAO phosphors. Applications of such flexible films in accurate ratiometric temperature sensing were also investigated.

Carboxylate nanofibrillated cellulose (ONFC) aqueous suspension with carboxyl substitution degree of 0.42 was purchased from the Hao Jia Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. A CNC-in-water suspension (0.5 wt% g/mL) was purchased from the University of Maine (ME, USA). The geometrical length and diameter of the CNCs were approximately 0.1–0.2 μm and 3.6–5.5 nm, respectively. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%), Hydrochloric acid (37%), and APTMS (97%) were purchased from Fisher Scientific, USA. The SrAl2O4: Eu2+, Dy3+ (SAO) phosphors were obtained from Lu Ming Co., Ltd., Dalian, China. CDs aqueous suspensions (0.5 mg/mL), synthesized from citric acid and organic amine, were purchased from the BeiDaJuBang Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China.

For the preparation of modified SAO phosphors, silica precursors were synthesized using a sol-gel method. Specifically, TEOS (1.2 mL) was mixed with aqueous ethanol (8.5 mL) in a 60 °C water bath. Hydrochloric acid was added to adjust the pH value to 3.0–3.5. CNC pre-dispersed CDs solutions were configured by adding 2.5 mL of alcohol and various amounts of CDs solution (1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, and 0.2 mL) to CNC aqueous suspensions (2.5 mL), respectively. Different CNC-CDs solutions (mass ratios of CNC-CDs solutions to SAO particles of 3.0, 2.5, 2.0, 1.5, and 1.0) were then mixed with the TEOS sol. Subsequently, SAO phosphors (1.2 g) were added and stirred for 15 min. The mixtures were washed using aqueous ethanol solution, then dried (70 °C) for 8 h to obtain modified particles, denoted as CNC-CDs@SAO. Meanwhile, control phosphors without CDs and CNC-CDs were fabricated utilizing similar methods and denoted as CNC@SiO2@SAO, and SiO2@SAO, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ONFC film with CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the fabrication of films, the CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors were modified with APTMS to change their surface characteristics (Fig. 1 ). The CNC-CDs@SAO particles (0.6 g) were mixed with APTMS (125 μL) and C2H5OH (45 g), and stirred for 8 h. The mixture was then washed with C2H5OH, and dried (75 °C) to obtain amino-coated particles denoted as NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO. Next, various amounts of the modified particles were added to 50 mL of ONFC dispersions (0.2 wt%). Fluorescent films were made utilizing a paper-making method (Zhang et al., 2018a). Finally, ONFC-based flat films (ONFC/NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO) with a thickness of 20–30 μm, denoted as directly ONFC sensing film, were obtained. For comparison, ONFC films with CNC-CDs@SAO particles were also prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of temperature-sensing films via solution dipping
ce:section.ce:para: To further enhance the CDs signals in films (Fig. 1), the ONFC/NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO films were immersed in a reconfigured CDs solution (0.2 mg/mL) with an alcohol/water volume ratio of 2/1 for 30 min. The films were then placed between two glass plates and dried at 40 °C. Finally, films with CDs impregnation, denoted as CDs-dipping ONFC sensing film, were obtained.

The morphologies were characterized utilizing Quanta-200 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 5.0 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss Libra 200 MC, Germany) images were recorded to analyze the coating layers of phosphors. Surface chemical element compositions were performed utilizing an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyzer (XPS, Thermo 250xi, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Spectrum One, USA) spectra were measured over a wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured from 10–80° at a scanning speed of 4°/min by an XRD meter (Rigaku, Japan). Zeta potentials were determined utilizing a Nano ZS Zetasizer (Malvern, UK) with water solvent. The pH for Zeta potential tests was not further adjusted by chemicals. Thermal analysis was processed by a Pyris-1 thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, Perkin Elmer, USA) in N2 atmosphere from 40 to 900 °C (20 °C/min). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. PL decay lifetime was recorded with a time-correlated module. The temperature-dependent PL emission spectra were measured by a fluorescence tester (Hitachi F-4500, Japan) connected to an affiliated heating apparatus. The light transmittance of the flexible ONFC films was measured utilizing a Cary 5000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Palo Alto, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of fluorescent phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and TEM micrographs of the SAO phosphors before and after the CNC pre-dispersed CDs (CNC-CDs)-coated modifications are presented in Fig. 2 . The surface of uncoated SAO phosphors is relatively smooth (Fig. 2a) compared to the coarse surface of the CNC-CDs and APTMS-grafted phosphors. For the CNC-CDs-coated phosphors (Fig. 2b), this phenomenon was probably the result of a silica coating layer with rod-shaped CNCs, where dispersed CDs were formed following the sol-gel modification. As shown in Fig. 2b, a thin coating containing rod-shaped CNCs and CDs was most likely formed on the surface of the SAO phosphors. Furthermore, the surface of the phosphors remains rough after the APTMS grafting modification (Fig. 2c). This is mainly because the reactive silicon hydroxyl groups grafted onto the SiO2-coated phosphors (Zhang, Lyu, Chen, & Wang, 2018b), which facilitated the deposition of APTMS on the CNC-CDs@SAO particles. However, further analyses were required to confirm the grafting of CNC-CDs on the SAO phosphors and APTMS on the CNC-CDs coated SAO particles.

The absorption peaks at 642, 770, and 850 cm−1 for the SAO phosphors (Fig. 3 a) correspond to the stretching vibration of the SrAl2O4 (Jamalaiah & Babu, 2018). These peaks also occurred in the modified SAO particles, indicating the luminescent center was not damaged by modifications. The peak near 3430 cm−1 in the spectra of modified phosphors is the stretching of −OH groups (Zhang et al., 2018a, 2018b). For CNC-CDs@SAO, a strong absorption at 1076 cm−1 was mainly attributed to the superimposed intensity resulting from asymmetric stretching of SiOSi groups and aromatic CH deformation vibration of CNC. Furthermore, the stretching vibration at 2924 cm−1 can be attributed to the CH groups, which are mainly derived from the CNC and CDs (Fig. 3b). For the NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO (Fig. 3a), there are no additional peaks compared to CNC-CDs@SAO, except for a small increase in the intensity at 1084 cm−1. These results indicate that amino-silane may react with the CNC-CDs@SAO (Pan, Barras, Boussekey, Addad, & Boukherroub, 2013). Furthermore, the grafting of reactive amino groups (–NH2) on the surface of phosphors was confirmed by the reversal Zeta potentials (Fig. 3d) ranging from −21.7 to + 18.6 mV following APTMS amino-modification.

The XRD patterns (Fig. 3c) for the modified particles showed no additional peaks, except for one wide band (2θ = 15–25°), compared to the crystal plane indexes of (011), (211), (220), (211), and (031) for the unmodified SAO phosphors (JCPDS 74-0794). The wide band centered at 2θ = 15–25° can be attributed to the CNC, CDs, and amorphous SiO2 (2θ = 22°, JCPDS 29-0085). This indicates that a SiO2 layer containing CNCs and CDs successfully crystallized on the surface of the SAO particles. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Zeta potential for the CNC and CDs was –33.9 and –11.4 mV, respectively. For the CDs, it was mainly attributed to the large numbers of amino (–NH2) and carboxyl groups (−COOH) on the surface of the nanoparticles (Gao, Ma et al., 2017). In the case of CNC, the strong negative characteristics mainly derived from the negative sulfate groups on the CNC due to the process of sulfuric acid oxidation. This also results in homogeneous suspensions when CNCs are introduced to disperse the CDs based on potential interactions between the specific crystalline phases of CNC and hydrophobic carbon structure of CDs (Olivier et al., 2012).

XPS was further utilized to confirm related surface element compositions and chemical bonding states after different coating modifications. Fig. 4 (a–d) present the XPS spectra for SAO and modified SAO particles, as well as the high-resolution N1s and Si 2p spectra for coated phosphors. As shown in Fig. 4a, the characteristic peaks (Al2p, Sr3d, Sr3p, and Sr3s) of SrAl2O4 disappeared for the modified phosphors, which is mainly due to the detection depth (2–10 nm) for XPS tests (Hirvikorpi, Vähä-Nissi, Mustonen, Iiskola, & Karppinen, 2010). Furthermore, the XPS results for the CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors exhibit four distinctive peaks centered on 103.2 eV (Si 2p), 154.8 eV (Si 2 s), 284.8 eV (C1s), and 400.8 eV (N1s), which implies the silica with dispersed CNCs and CDs successfully crystallized on the surface of the SAO phosphors. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. 4b) for the CNC-CDs@SAO particles reveal two nitrogen-related features located at 398.6 eV and 400.8 eV, which can be attributed to pyridine N and amino N from the CDs, respectively (Yu et al., 2018). These two peaks for N1s are related to the detection of partial CDs in the coating layer based on XPS inspection.

Furthermore, the signal of pyridine-N corresponding to the CDs disappeared and the relative intensity of nitrogen in the coated phosphors increased following APTMS modification (Fig. 4c). This result indicates that APTMS tends to experience grafting reactions on the CNC-CDs@SAO particles and enhance the signal of amino-N. The Si 2p XPS spectra of the modified phosphors in Fig. 4d reveal silicon at 103.2 eV originating from the Si–O–Si linkage. The counts of Si 2p for the APTMS-modified phosphors were enhanced compared to the CNC-CDs@SAO particles (Fig. 4d). This confirms the successful grafting of APTMS onto core-shell phosphors via the covalent bonding of siloxane with the increased hydroxyl groups on the CNC-CDs@SAO particles.

The dominant compositions of the coatings with CNC pre-dispersed CDs and APTMS were confirmed based on their thermal decomposition behavior by TGA (Fig. 4e). The degradation rate of the uncoated SAO particles retained unchanged from 40 to 850 °C. This can be attributed to the good thermal stability of the SrAl2O4 particles (Mothudi, Ntwaeaborwa, Kumar, Sohn, & Swart, 2012). In contrast, both modified phosphors experienced rapid mass loss of 2.0% at 120 °C based on the elimination of absorbed H2O. Additionally, an intense weight loss (1.46%) occurred in 220–400 °C. This was caused by the thermal degradation of CNCs and CDs, which confirms that the ratio of the CNCs and CDs in the layers is about 1.0–2.0% (Meng et al., 2015). Furthermore, the DTG curve (Fig. 4f) for NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO shows a degradation peak at 400–560 °C, which is caused by the pyrolysis of amino-polymers that chemically grafted on the surface of CNC-CDs@SAO particles. The mass loss at 850 °C for CNC-CDs@SAO and NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors was calculated to be 5.18% and 7.63%, respectively. Therefore, the mass ratio of APTMS grafted on CNC-CDs@SAO was estimated to be 2.45% of the phosphors.

Fig. 5 a presents the excitation spectrum of the SAO particles and emission wavelength of the CDs. There is an apparent optical overlap between the CDs emission and excitation spectrum of the SAO phosphors, indicating that a potential energy-transfer process may occur from the CDs to SAO. Fig. 5b presents the PL spectra of the SAO phosphors, CDs, and CNC-CDs@SAO particles (irritated at 365-nm). It is clear that the coated particles (CNC-CDs@SAO) exhibit an integrated spectrum including the two substrates of CDs and SAO. The normalized fluorescence intensity of CNC-CDs@SAO and coated phosphors without CNC-assisted dispersion is shown in Fig. S1. Furthermore, the optical signal of the CDs in the coated CNC-CDs@SAO particles is enhanced by increasing the loading of CDs (Fig. 5c).

The relative fluorescence intensity of the emission spectra (Fig. 5d) were 6.91% and 10.3% lower for CNC-CDs@SAO and NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO particles, respectively, compared to that of the uncoated SAO. This is ascribed to the optical scattering behavior caused by the CNC-CDs, silica, and APTMS grafting layers. Furthermore, the spectrum shape was broadened by the CDs incorporation. For instance, the emission corresponding to the half-peak intensity of the modified particles increased approximately by 45.5% from 90-nm to 165-nm compared to the uncoated SAO. Specifically, fluorescent release over a wider wavelength increases the collection of optical signals, which provides greater potential for stable signal monitoring.

Fluorescent energy transfer typically refers to the process from an excited phase to a proximate receptor through a non-radiative pattern (Chen, Liu, Tsai, & Yeh, 2018; Long, Li, Zhang, & Yao, 2015). Fig. 6 a presents the transfer mechanism of the modified phosphors and the potential process through an energy band structure. One of the main functions of the rod-shaped CNCs in this system is that they facilitate the dispersion of CDs and mitigate the fluorescent quenching caused by the agglomeration of CDs (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the rod-shaped CNCs, which are dispersed in the coating layer, promote the propagation of photo signals with wide-angle scattering (Fig. S2) and enhance the capture ability of the SAO, thereby promoting the stability of the dual-emission.

Fluorescence decay lifetime refers to the average time required for excited-state electrons from excited state to a ground state (Gao, Cui et al., 2017). The decay curves of the CNC-CDs@SAO samples in comparison to CNC-coated SAO and SiO2-coated SAO particles (SiO2@SAO) were presented to prove the CNCs can improve the energy-transfer process. The decay curves (Fig. 6b) can be accurately fitted by a second-order exponential model as follows:
(1)
I(t) = I0 + A1 exp(－t / τ1) + A2 exp(－t / τ2)
where I(t) is the fluorescent intensity at time t, I0 is the base intensity, τ1 and τ2 are the lifetimes of the exponential components, A1 and A2 are constants, and the average decay lifetime (τ) is calculated (Yu, Li, Zeng, Zheng, & Wu, 2013) according the following Eq. (2):
(2) τ = (A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2)/(A1τ1 + A2τ2)

The decay time of the SiO2-coated SAO, CNC-coated SAO, and CNC-CDs@SAO particles was calculated as 2.19, 2.52, and 3.24 ns, respectively. Theoretically, the decay time of the SAO acceptors would increase when an energy-transfer behavior occurs from the CDs to SAO. It is evident that the incorporation of CNCs increased the decay time from 2.19 to 2.52 ns, which confirms that the dispersed CNCs optimized the energy-transfer process via light scattering. Furthermore, the decay time of the CNC-CDs-coated particles was slightly increased (1.05 ns) compared to the CNC@SiO2@SAO without CDs. This indicates that an additional energy-transfer process occurred between the CNC-dispersed CDs in the shell layer and SAO core. Fig. 6c presents a CIE chromaticity diagram for the modified SAO, CDs, and CNC-CDs@SAO samples under 370-nm excitation. The luminescent color of samples numbered from 1# through 5# showed a striking blue-shift and turned from green (0.225, 0.408) to blue-green (0.214, 0.317) with increasing CNC-CDs content. These optical properties indicate that the green color of SrAl2O4 phosphors can be tuned to blue-green by altering the content of blue CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of fluorescent hybrid ONFC sensing films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 presents the surface and cross-section SEM morphologies of an ONFC film, as well as the sensing films with coated phosphors (ONFC/phosphors = 5/5). Compared to the flat surface of ONFC film, the surface morphologies of the sensing films (Fig. 7(b, c)) were rough because of the dispersed phosphors. The amino-grafted particles were compactly wrapped by the ONFC and dispersed in the scaffold of nanofibers (Fig. 7f). However, the film with CNC-CDs@SAO particles presented a more detached structure and contained more gaps at the interface between the substrate and phosphors. This is mainly because of the poor activity of the inorganic silica. Furthermore, the reactive amino-grafted phosphors with a positive charge (+18.6 mV) were liable to produce electrostatic adherence with the ONFC (−46.8 mV), which is liable to facilitate better phase interfaces for the as-prepared films. Fig. 7(g–i) present the schematic models of the pure ONFC film and dual-emitting fluorescence films with distinct modified SAO particles.

To prepare isotropic flexible sensing films, homogenous luminescent dispersions were of crucial importance for the modified SAO phosphors. Fig. 8 a presents photographs of the SAO, CNC-CDs@SAO, and NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO phosphor samples blended with H2O and ONFC substrate. Both the coated and unmodified particles tended to sink in H2O. However, the phosphors with coating modifications retained good luminescence in water compared to the fluorescent quenching caused by hydrolysis for the uncoated SAO phosphors. As shown in the SEM and FTIR analysis, the formed silica coating layer on SAO phosphors restrained aqueous solution from permeating in the crystalline SAO skeleton, resulting enhanced water resistance.

However, both the modified particles were esay-to-mix in the ONFC dispersion and uniform suspensions were observed (Fig. 8a). This is mainly due to the mutual hydrogen bonds between the cellulose and CNC-CDs@SAO particles, as well as additional electrostatic adsorption for the NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO particles based on opposite surface charges (Junka, Guo, Filpponen, Laine, & Rojas, 2014). Additionally, sufficient cellulose nanofibrils and their strong electrostatic repulsion resulting from surface carboxyl groups ensured the stability of the mixed dispersions.

The ONFC hybrid films with different loading of phosphors showed good translucence with visible letters under the films (Fig. 8a). The optical transparency of the ONFC film and sensing films is presented in Fig. 8b. The ONFC film showed a high transmittance of 85.7% at a 700-nm wavelength. This can be attributed to the weak light-scatting effect of the nanostructure skeletons, where most light can transmit through the nanofibrils (Miao et al., 2015). Compared to the ONFC film, the transmittance of the sensing films decreased dramatically from 85.7% to 10.8–18.2%, which is due to the light-scattering behavior of the SAO particles. Compared to the strong transmission of the ONFC film (Fig. 8c), the laser-scattering behaviors of the sensing film (Fig. 8d) provided hazy results. However, this haze behavior is actually favorable for providing broad optical beams and more uniform light signals. Additionally, the highly transparent ONFC substrate facilitates light propagation and causes the luminescent units to receive greater optical signal intensity (Fig. 8e), which improves the detection behavior of fluorescent signals.

Fig. 9 a presents the fluorescent temperature-dependent emission spectra of the ONFC sensing films with NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors (ONFC/phosphor = 5/5), detected under 243–383 K with a single 370-nm excitation. This excitation source was selected because the CDs can release relatively strong fluorescent intensity under such UV excitation conditions (Fu et al., 2015). Furthermore, the strong fluorescent signals of the CDs in the shell structure are more likely to transmit energy to the SrAl2O4 core, thereby maximizing the dual-emission signals. As shown in Fig. 9a, the overall fluorescent emission intensity decreased with an increasing temperature from 243 to 383 K. This was a result of the thermal quenching effects of most luminescent materials (Kim, Park, Kim, Choi, & Park, 2005; Wu et al., 2015).

Numerous investigations have suggested that heat-induced quantum tunneling can occur with an increase in temperature (Kim et al., 2005). Additionally, the SAO emission of the dual-emissive fluorescence showed a slight blue shift at higher temperatures. This can be attributed to the enhanced 5d electron population and emission energy at higher-energy sublevels resulting from phonon-assisting effects at increased temperatures (Valerini et al., 2005). In contrast, a minor red shift was observed for the CDs emission signals near 452-nm. It was mainly due to higher temperatures promoted the non-radiant channels of surface defect (or trap) states, thereby decreasing PL emissions (Eda et al., 2010). The decrease of the CDs fluorescent intensity was much gentler compared to that of the SAO emission. The tunable red shift of the CDs and obvious blue shift of the SAO optical signal were also present for the ONFC/NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO sensing film and CDs-dipping ONFC sensing film (Fig. 9b). For the CDs-dipping ONFC sensing film, the biggest difference was that the blue light signal of the CDs (near 452-nm) was significantly enhanced as a result of the numerous CDs attached on the ONFC surface through the dipping process. The dominant fluorescent signal of the SAO (near 506-nm) in the sensing film tended to attenuate with increasing temperatures as a result of the faster luminescent quenching of the SAO emission compared to that of CDs. The relative ratio relationships between the emission intensities of the SAO (506-nm) and CDs (452-nm) with varying temperature are presented in Fig. 9c. A strong linear correlation was obtained in 243–383 K as the temperature increased. This linearity can be defined utilizing the following equations:
(3)
T1 = 478.62 – 389.11 PL506/PL452
(4)
T2 = 378.81 – 370.37 PL506/PL452,
where PL506 and PL452 are the corresponding PL emission signal intensities under the wavelengths of 506-nm and 452-nm, respectively. T1 and T2 are the detection temperatures (°C) for the ONFC sensing films and CDs-dipping ONFC sensing films, respectively. The correlation coefficients of these two systems were calculated to be 0.990 and 0.985, respectively.

To further exhibit the temperature-dependent reversibility of the films (Fig. 9d), cycles were repeatedly executed for the two different types of ONFC sensing films in 243–383 K. The results reveal relatively good cycling performance, which illustrates the reusable characteristics of the sensing films for temperature monitoring between 243 and 383 K. This is mainly attributed to the recovery of PL intensity for the CDs and SAO phosphors (Lei et al., 2015) when the heat-induced quenching effect was released by cooling the composite films.

For ratiometric fluorescent temperature sensor, the relative sensitivity (S) is typically calculated (Cadiau et al., 2013) utilizing the following equation:
(5)
S = (∂△/ ∂T)/△,
where △ is the PL intensity ratio of the dual-emission under the wavelengths of the SAO and CDs signals. Here, the sensitivities of ONFC/NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO sensing film and CDs-dipping ONFC sensing film were calculated to be 0.257% K−1 and 0.270% K−1 at 383 K, respectively. This is a result of the excellent linear correlation between temperature and fluorescent intensity. Both the ONFC sensing film and CDs-dipping ONFC sensing films showed good linear responses under varying temperature, indicating the ONFC films with NH2@CNC-CDs@SAO phosphors are reliable and useful fluorescence thermometers.

A simple and effective core-shell coating strategy with CNCs and CDs was implemented to create excellent scattered dual-emission signals and to improve the water resistance of SAO phosphors through a facile one-step sol-gel method. An optical dual-emitting spectrum was observed under a single 365-nm irritation and the optical signals of the phosphors could be controlled by altering the CDs content. Furthermore, APTMS amino-grafting modification enhanced the surface activity of the phosphors and facilitated better phase interfaces for the as-prepared flexible ONFC films through a paper-making process. The fluorescent emissions for both the hybrid ONFC sensing film and the CDs-dipping ONFC sensing film were highly sensitive to surrounding temperature variations (243–383 K). Additionally, strong linear behavior was obtained for such sensitive sensors. Such flexible and soft nanocellulose sensing films with strong temperature responses, strong light-induced scattering, and good flexibility are excellent prospective candidates for temperature sensor devices in harsh industrial applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds of the Chinese Academy of Forestry for Wood Composites and Chemical Utilization Program (CAFYBB2017ZX003), as well as the USDA National Institute of Food and Agriculture (Hatch Project 1012359). The authors also acknowledge financial support from the China Scholarship Council.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2018.11.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
